https://github.com/conda-forge/miniforge

conda config --add channels defaults
conda config --add channels bioconda
conda config --add channels conda-forge
conda config --set channel_priority strict

`\t` tab-delimited format is the most common
`\n` linefeed character (Linux and OS), Windows (\r\n)`

head -n 3 file.txt
tail -n +2 text.txt
(head -n 2; tail -n 2) < file.txt
 # inspect the first and last 3 lines of a file
i() { (head -n 2; tail -n 2) < "$1" | column -t}

# Commands for less
space - next page
b - previous page
g - first
G - Last line
j - down one 
k - up one line 
/<pattern> - search forward
?<pattern> - search backward
n - repeat last search (downward)
N - repeat last search (backward)

a complex pipeline with bugs | less (this will stop the pipeline to visualiza the output)

be careful with wc - l (it doesnt care about empty lines)

grep -c "[^\\n\\t]" text.txt
# To obtain the number of columns after the 5 comments rows
tail -n +5 | awk - F "\t" '{print NF; exit}' 
## this is more robust (grep is the fastest)
grep -v "^#" | awk - F "\t" '{print NF; exit}' 

cut -f 1-4
# but it wont order it
cut -f 1,3 
# treat the output as a table
column -t 
grep "pattern" --color=auto
grep -v -w the notebook
# For extended regular expressions
grep - E 

BSD utils - OS (brew install coreutils)
GNU coreutils - Linux

# ASCII-encoded
file file.txt
hexdump -c improperfile.txt
LC_CTYPE=C grep --color='auto' -P "[\x80-\xFF]" improper.fa
alias nonascii="LC_CTYPE=C grep --color='auto' -n -P '[\x80-\xFF]'"

sort | uniq

sort -k1,1 -k2,2n example.bed

sort -k1,1 -k2,2n -c example_sorted.bed
sort -k1,1V # for chromosomes or alphanumeric
echo $?

join -1 1 -2 1 example_sorted.bed example_lengths.txt > example_with_lengths.txt

# unpaired
join -1 1 -2 1 -a 1 example_sorted.bed example_lengths_alt.txt # GNU join only

# AWK (better to use gawk for GNU)

one record at a time (one line)
the entire record is $0

pattern { action }
"""
a == b
a != b
a < b 
a > b
a <= b
a >= b
a ~ b # matches regular expression
a && b 
a || b 

"""
awk '$1 ~ /chr2|chr3/ { print $0 "\t" $3 - $2 }' example.bed

awk 'BEGIN{ s = 0 }; { s += ($3-$2) }; END{ print "mean: " s/NR };' example.bed
NR: number of records that were read
set the record (RS), output field (OFS), and out‐ put record (ORS) separators.
awk -v VAR=val

awk -F"," -v OFS="\t" {print $1,$2,$3}
awk 'NR >= 3 && NR <= 5' example.bed

awk '/Lypla1/ { feature[$3] += 1 }; \
        END { for (k in feature)          \
        print k "\t" feature[k] }' Mus_musculus.GRCm38.75_chr1.gtf

awk functions:
length(s)
tolower(s)
toupper(s)
substr(s, i, j)
split(s, x, d)
sub(f, r, s)

Somatic vs germline variants
SNV and Indels

Structural variation

- Deletion
- Duplication
- Inversion 
- Insertion
- Translocation


Somatic Variant Calling and Annotation. Somatic variants were identified using GATK Mutect2 and subjected to a multi-stage quality control and filtering pipeline. Raw variant calls were processed using GATK VariantFiltration with ten empirically-derived hard filters to exclude sequencing and alignment artifacts, consistent with established best practices for somatic variant discovery (Van der Auwera et al., 2013; Cibulskis et al., 2013). Variants were filtered based on allele frequency (AF < 0.01), allelic depth in tumor sample (AD < 5 reads), sequencing depth (DP < 20x), strand bias (Fisher Strand score >25.0), somatic evidence strength (Tumor Log-Odds ratio <3.0), and sequence quality metrics including base quality (MBQ < 25), mapping quality (MQ < 30), and read position bias (ReadPosRankSum < -5.0). Additionally, variants with evidence of sample contamination (CONTQ < 20), potential germline contamination (GERMQ < 20), or duplicate artifacts (AS_UNIQ_ALT_READ_COUNT < 2) were marked as low-confidence. Fragment length bias, a sensitive indicator of systematic sequencing errors (Poplin et al., 2016), was assessed by comparing insert size distributions between reference and alternate alleles (|MFRL[tumor] - MFRL[normal]| > 100). High-confidence somatic variants passing these filters were then annotated using GATK Funcotator with functional effect prediction and cross-reference to external databases including ClinVar, gnomAD, and dbSNP, using BEST_EFFECT transcript selection to assign a single, biologically most consequential annotation per variant. The final variant call set consisted of variants with PASS status in the VCF file, representing high-confidence somatic mutations suitable for clinical interpretation or downstream analysis. This multi-tiered filtering approach substantially reduces false-positive variant calls while maintaining sensitivity for detecting true somatic alterations, as demonstrated in benchmark studies evaluating GATK's somatic variant discovery performance across diverse tumor types (Cibulskis et al., 2013; Garrison & Marth, 2012).
